description: Plural 'lo' makes an otherwise unquantified noun explicitly more than one. A bare noun leaves number to context, while a numeral or quantifier makes its own quantity claim.
articulatory_notes: Voiced air passes around the sides of the tongue for alveolar 'l'. The tongue then draws back as the lips round for true-mid 'o', with stress on the whole form.
sound_symbolism: The lateral opens into rounded 'o' with no closure between them. That broad release can suggest several referents gathered under one noun.
usage_notes: A noun phrase takes 'lo' only when it has neither a numeral nor a quantifier. The particle gives no finer count than plurality and triggers no agreement on the verb. In the Slot 2 cline it follows a deictic and precedes focus or degree.
